reasoning.A:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My puzzle is trivial (44 + 29 = 73), so I’m fully confident my own answer is correct. In all previous games, every puzzle has been simple arithmetic and there have never been conflicting capsules—whenever anyone entered the password, it was correct. That makes the risk that B, C, or D are wrong extremely small compared to the 1‑in‑4 chance of dying if nobody volunteers and a random player is killed. Verifying would just cost a coin and still leave the random‑death risk if others happened to pass. By volunteering immediately with Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D, I both preserve all 4 coins and ensure there is at least one volunteer, giving the best chance for everyone (including me) to survive.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: All puzzles so far have been simple arithmetic, and every initial capsule has always been correct; the only death came from random elimination when nobody volunteered. My own puzzle (36 ÷ 4) is trivial, so I’m very confident my capsule is right, and there is exactly one capsule per question with no conflicts, making an incorrect 4-part password extremely unlikely. Passing keeps the risk that no one volunteers and we face random death, and verifying would just burn a coin without materially improving safety. Volunteering now with each player’s own capsule is therefore the best way to avoid random elimination and almost certainly escape with all 4 coins.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: So far across many games, every puzzle has been simple arithmetic, there’s never been a conflict between capsules, and every initial single-answer set (Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D) has produced a correct password. My own puzzle (32 ÷ 4) is trivial and I’m fully confident my sealed answer is right, and past evidence strongly suggests the others are as well.

Spending 1 coin to verify only one question gives very little extra safety while still leaving the remaining questions unverified, and fully checking all others would be too costly relative to the tiny perceived error risk. Passing risks the situation where everyone waits and a random player dies. Given the strong track record and low difficulty of the puzzles, the probability that this full password is wrong is much lower than the risk of random death if nobody volunteers, so volunteering now with Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D maximizes my expected survival while preserving all 4 coins if we escape.
reasoning.D:
DECISION: Pass
REASONING: I am a dummy agent and always pass.

rounds.outcome:
